claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance; Child has started an apprenticeship in England
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
Child has started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

Let's start with the basics:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Yes, I have one child. I'd like to know if I'm eligible to claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! I can help you with that. Let me gather some information about your situation.

**What is your child's name, and how old are they?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: My child's name is Alex, and they're 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 17 years old, I need to ask a follow-up question:

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, studying at school, college, or in an approved training program)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex recently started an apprenticeship, so they're in training for that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to clarify an important detail:

**Is Alex's apprenticeship specifically an apprenticeship in England?** (This is important as the rules differ for apprenticeships in England compared to other parts of the UK)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - the apprenticeship is in England.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. I have one more important question:

**Do you live with Alex in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. Alex lives at home with me, and we live in the UK - we're in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, I have completed my assessment:

**Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why: Although Alex is only 17 years old and lives at home with you in the UK, the rules specifically state that you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts an apprenticeship in England. Since Alex has recently started an apprenticeship in England, your eligibility for Child Benefit has ended.

This rule applies even though Alex is under 20 and still living with you. The start of an apprenticeship in England is one of the specific circumstances that stops Child Benefit entitlement.

If you were previously receiving Child Benefit for Alex, you should inform HMRC about this change in circumstances, as the payments should have stopped when the apprenticeship began.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about this decision?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and has started an apprenticeship in England. According to the Child Benefit rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts an apprenticeship in England. Even though Alex is under 20 and still lives at home with the claimant in the UK, the specific rule about apprenticeships in England means that Child Benefit eligibility ends when the apprenticeship starts. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives with their 17-year-old child Alex in Manchester, UK. While Alex is under 20 and lives at home, they have recently started an apprenticeship in England. Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility stops when a child starts an apprenticeship in England, regardless of their age or living situation. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and has started an apprenticeship in England. According to the Child Benefit rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts an apprenticeship in England. Even though Alex is under 20 and still lives at home with the claimant in the UK, the specific rule about apprenticeships in England means that Child Benefit eligibility ends when the apprenticeship starts. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives with their 17-year-old child Alex in Manchester, UK. While Alex is under 20 and lives at home, they have recently started an apprenticeship in England. Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility stops when a child starts an apprenticeship in England, regardless of their age or living situation. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
